data.title: VBR: Small-Cap Value Stocks Can Offer Differentiated Exposure To Underrepresented Sectors
data.description:
The Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR) is a low-cost, passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to track the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index. Small-cap value companies may offer investors differentiated returns with respect to larger indices that may be more heavily exposed to the technology sector, particularly given the high concentration across the Magnificent 7 stocks.

About Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF

VBR was launched by The Vanguard Group on January 26, 2004 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. VBR is a low-cost investment strategy with an expense ratio of 7bps. The fund has roughly $58b in net assets with an average of $65.5mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity for actively managing a position.

VBR provides a modest quarterly distribution annualized at $4.17/share, yielding 2.07% on a trailing twelve-month basis. VBR has grown its distribution rate for four consecutive years and has consistently paid out a distribution over the last 20 years.

VBR tracks the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index. The Index is a market cap-weighted index designed to track the performance of small-cap value-oriented stocks and has a weighted median market cap of $8.75b. The Index is broadly diversified across 823 constituents with the top 10 companies having an aggregate weight of 6.06%. The Index provides diversified exposure across sectors with the largest concentration being in industrials at 21.8%, financials at 20.9%, and consumer discretionary at 9% [as of September 30, 2025].

VBR has 842 stocks that make up the portfolio with an aggregate price/earnings ratio of 17.6x. By comparison, the Vanguard large-cap value ETF (VTV) has an aggregate price/earnings multiple of 20.6x. Top holdings within the portfolio include NRG Energy (NRG) at 0.86%, EMCOR Group (EME) at 0.78%, and Sandisk Corp (SNDK) at 0.71%. Given that the portfolio is broadly diversified with minimal company concentration, I do not believe any one stock will significantly impact the performance of the strategy.

Comparing small-cap funds, VBR is the lowest-cost strategy with an expense ratio of 7bps.

Looking at the income component, VBR provides a stronger distribution yield relative to peer small-cap strategies. VBR has also exhibited stronger distribution growth in recent years, potentially providing investors with a more appealing investment outlay.

By comparison, VBR will provide the broadest diversification across companies as well as the lowest company concentration amongst the top 10 holdings. This can cushion the portfolio from the performance of any single company and potentially smooth out volatility risk.

In terms of performance, VBR has largely outperformed most peers in recent years with the exception of the Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV), which is an actively-managed small-cap strategy.

Investor Suitability

VBR can be utilized as a core holding for US small-cap value equities as well as a satellite position as part of a diversified portfolio strategy to gain exposure to underrepresented sectors like industrials.

VBR can potentially provide investors with differentiated returns relative to larger stock indices like the S&P 500 (SPY), which is heavily weighted in the information technology sector. With the S&P 500 exhibiting substantial concentration in the Magnificent 7 stocks, VBR can be utilized as a diversifier to gain exposure across other sectors. The downside risk is that VBR may not provide significant exposure to high-growth sectors like communications and technology that may outperform on a price-return basis. Overall, VBR can be best utilized for diversification and differentiated returns.

Comparing total returns across the Vanguard small-cap value and large-cap value ETFs, VTV has generally outperformed VBR across most holding periods. I suspect that this phenomenon may be tied to larger companies having more access to capital to grow operations, providing substantially more flexibility through the economic cycles.

Small-cap stocks can potentially outperform during interest rate easing cycles, as experienced in recent months following the Federal Reserve’s rate cuts in September and October 2025. I believe small-cap companies may be more sensitive to interest rate policy given the easing cost of capital as it relates to financing operations. Easing rates can provide small-caps with more financial cushion to the bottom line, particularly if refinancing debt at a lower rate. The hazard small-caps face is the rationale behind the rate-easing cycle, whether it’s the result of easing inflation or economic hardship. The recent challenge is that most economic data wasn’t reported throughout the recent government shutdown, providing little insight into the health of the US economy. This means that metrics that are used to gauge economic performance like retail sales and nonfarm payrolls were not reported in recent weeks, making it more challenging to evaluate expectations for sectors that may be sensitive to these metrics. If the US economy were to be going through a rough patch, I believe small-caps may be most exposed to economic hardship, potentially resulting in a negative impact to their respective share prices.

Risks Related to VBR

VBR is a passively managed, small-cap value ETF, exposing investors to certain risks that must be considered prior to making an investment decision. Small-cap stocks may be relatively more sensitive to economic cycles with respect to large-cap stocks, potentially exposing investors to significant downside risk. VBR is passively managed, meaning that the portfolio will not take a defensive position during periods of market stress. VBR has limited exposure to growth-oriented sectors, potentially limiting upside potential relative to large-cap indices.

Final Thoughts

VBR can be an appealing small-cap value strategy for value investors seeking differentiated exposure to underrepresented sectors like industrials or consumer staples. Compared to peer small-cap value ETFs, VBR has exhibited strong performance with a low expense ratio of 7bps, making it an appealing investment strategy for those seeking exposure to the investment cohort. Given the relative performance of VBR, I am recommending the ETF with a Buy rating with a 2-4% allocation target.

data.title: U.S. Economy: The Scary Part Of Rising Loan Delinquencies
data.description:
Today, we circle back on the health of the overall U.S. consumer. After all, nearly 70% of U.S. GDP is driven by consumer activity. The top 10% of households by income continue to hold up quite well. They now account for roughly 50% of all consumer spending in the country, up substantially from approximately 35% two generations ago -- one of the side effects of globalization among many other factors. This cohort will likely continue to prosper as long as the stock and real estate markets don't crack noticeably and job growth stays positive.

The rest of the consumer base is where deep issues are developing. This helps explain why consumer sentiment is in the tank despite equities near all-time highs and near 4% GDP growth in the second and third quarters of this year.

You can see the increasing angst and stress within the vast majority of the population by looking at rising loan delinquencies in just about every segment of finance. This weakness is starting to impact results from more consumer discretionary names that depend on the "mass market." Home Depot (HD) fell 6% on Tuesday after reporting mixed Q3 results and offering soft forward guidance. Other names like Starbucks (SBUX) and Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), among other well-known names, have posted tepid Q3 results as well. Target (TGT) reported third-quarter results Wednesday morning before the bell, that showed same-store sales fell nearly 3% from the third quarter of 2024.

Consumer stress is starting to have increasingly dire outcomes. Chapter 7 bankruptcy filings have risen 15% through the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024. In September, chapter 7 bankruptcy filings were up 19% year-over-year.

After a nearly four-year taxpayer hiatus, repayments and collection procedures have restarted on student loan debt this year. This has predictably caused student loan delinquencies to soar. Student loans have also started to be reported to the credit bureaus again. This has significantly reduced the credit scoresfor millions of Americans. This reduces the potential buying pool for big-ticket items like autos, homes, boats, RVs, and appliances.

Speaking of auto loans, outside the prime segment, things are getting dire in this loan segment. The United States is tracking 3 million auto repossessions in 2025. That hasn't happened since 2009, during the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. Fitch Ratings reported that in October, subprime auto loans that were 60 days or greater past due hit 6.65%. That is a record delinquency level. The concerning part of this story is usually the last two bills that consumers don't pay are their rent/mortgage payment and their auto loan payment. This is an ominous sign around the health of the consumer.

Credit card debt now stands at just over $1.2 trillion, an all-time record. As can be seen in the chart above, credit card loan delinquency and charge-off rates are far lower than during the GFC. However, they are surging and now significantly above pre-pandemic levels and continuing to rise.

Finally, increasing consumer stress is a huge factor behind the explosive growth of BNPL or Buy Now Pay Later. A financing option that first appeared in 2019 and is now utilized by approximately one in five Americans. The average BNPL balance is now just under $2,100. This should be raising red flags, as it isn't exactly a picture of consumer health, when a good slice of the consumer population is financing the purchase of a pizza. Pun not intended.

Given the deteriorating state of most consumers in the United States, I remain heavily underweight the consumer discretionary sector within my portfolio. I am also watching what is happening to the top 10% of consumers closely. This well-off cohort is the only one keeping consumer spending from going negative and most likely pushing the U.S. economy into recession.

The scary part of fast-rising loan delinquencies is that this is happening despite near 4% GDP growth over the past two quarters and when job growth still remains positive despite slowing significantly from the first half of 2025. What happens when job growth goes negative and GDP growth falls substantially? My take is investors are not going to like the answer to that question.

data.title: Crescent Capital BDC: 13% Yield And 31% Discount Make It A Buy
data.description:
Rewards and risks go hand in hand, and that goes for virtually all investments. For example, a quality asset is only a good investment if bought at a good price. This means that investors who overpay are at risk of subpar returns due to years of growth already priced into the stock.

At the other end of the spectrum, some stocks become cheap to the point that market pessimism is already baked into the stock. This pushes the dividend yield to a high amount, thereby compensating investors well for holding onto the stock.

This brings me to Crescent Capital BDC (CCAP), which I last covered in August, highlighting its conservatively managed portfolio and improving dividend coverage. CCAP continues to trade at a cheap price far under its NAV. At $13.33, CCAP sits near the bottom of its 52-week range while carrying a 13% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit CCAP including recent business results, and discuss what makes it an attractive ‘Buy’ for its high yield and total returns, so let’s get started!

Why CCAP?

Crescent Capital is a BDC that’s externally managed by Crescent Capital Group, which brings a multi-decade track record to managing credit markets. CCAP provides debt financing solutions to private equity sponsor-backed companies in the U.S. (88% of investments), Europe (10%) and Australia (2%).

The portfolio remains conservatively managed with 90% of it being first-lien senior secured and unitranche first lien investments. It carries a diversified portfolio with a $1.58 billion fair value spread across 187 companies and 18 industries. As shown below, top industries are defensive in nature with healthcare, software, commercial services, consumer services and insurance being the top 5 industries, representing 78% of portfolio value.

CCAP demonstrated sound results, with Q3 2025 NII per share falling slightly by $0.02 on a sequential basis from $0.48 to $0.46. Notably, this remains higher than $0.45 NII/share it achieved in Q1 of this year. This means that the $0.42 base dividend remains covered at a 109.5% NII-to-Dividend coverage ratio. Plus, for added protection to the dividend, CCAP has $1.10 in spillover income per share.

Yield on income-producing securities was 10.4% during Q3, which was unchanged on a QoQ basis. Encouragingly, non-accruals improved from a recent high of 2.4% to 1.6% on a sequential basis. Portfolio companies have a solid weighted average interest coverage ratio of 2.1x.

NAV per share declined sequentially from $19.55 to $19.28. This was due to pressures on valuations stemming from tariffs, particularly at its European portfolio companies. As shown below, despite recent pressure on NAV per share, it remains higher than the $19.13 level from 10 years ago.

At the same time, CCAP deployed $74 million, including $51 million on 7 new investments and $22 million toward add-ons. This compares against $86 million in portfolio exits and repayments, resulting in around $12 million in portfolio contraction for the quarter.

Spreads on new originations remained at a healthy 450 to 500 basis points over SOFR for the core lower middle market that CCAP targets. This implies that it hasn’t had to compromise for lower yields on investments in a competitive environment for deals.

Looking ahead, management continues to stress the importance of credit quality via sponsor-backed companies. Having a private equity partner helps to ensure a another level of due diligence and strategic assistance for the portfolio companies. Moreover, CCAP plans to expand non-interest income (i.e. origination/structuring fees) as a growth lever beyond traditional loans. Plus, lower interest rates could stimulate deal activity, as noted during the recent earnings call:

A more accommodative rate environment should serve as a tailwind for new deal activity. Lower borrowing costs are expected to support renewed M&A and refinancing volumes, creating opportunities for attractive reinvestment and additional fee income. We are optimistic that over time, we may see higher levels of noninterest-related income as compared to this third quarter, driven by a pickup in origination and structuring fees on new investments as well as accelerated amortizations on realizations.

CCAP carries a solid balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.20x, which sits inside its target leverage of 1.1x to 1.3x. This also sits comfortably under the 2.0x statutory limit for BDCs.

Lastly, I find CCAP to be a bargain at the current price of $13.33, which equates to a price-to-book value of just 0.69x. As shown below, this sits close to the bottom of CCAP’s valuation range since its inception in February of 2020.

At a 31% discount to book value, the market appears to have baked in too much pessimism, especially considering the drop in non-accruals during Q3. CCAP’s valuation also sits far below that of peers Ares Capital (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), as shown below. As such, with a 12.6% regular dividend yield, investors are getting a solid margin of safety with CCAP at the current price for potentially strong total returns.

Risks to CCAP include its sensitivity to interest rate changes, as 97% of its debt investments are floating rate. As such, a faster-than-expected drop in rates could result in investment spread compression before it’s able to refinance balance sheet debt at lower rates.

Plus, CCAP trading at below NAV means that it can’t issue equity to fund growth. Lastly, CCAP has 13% of its portfolio on watch list, which management identifies as those having internal ratings of 3, 4 or 5. This is on a scale from 1-5, with 1 being the highest quality. As such, this is worth monitoring over the coming quarters.

Investor Takeaway

CCAP looks like a high-yield opportunity that the market has pushed too far down. The portfolio remains conservatively managed and dividend coverage remains solid with spillover income. Plus, CCAP’s non-accrual rate improved on a sequential basis.

CCAP’s shares are trading at a sizable 31% discount from book value while offering 12.6% yield. As such, investors get paid well to wait for sentiment to turn and deal activity to pick up in a lower-rate environment. While risks remain for the company, the discounted price tilts the balance in favor of reward on CCAP at present.

data.title: Agios Pharma: Study Fail May Affect Mitapivat TDT Approval - Expect More Volatility
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Agios Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (AGIO) has hit the skids in trading today - down 49% at the time of writing, trading at $23 per share, which translates to a market cap valuation of ~$1.4bn.

The losses appear to have been triggered by the release today of data from a Phase 3 study of Agios' (AGIO) drug candidate mitapivat in patients with Sickle Cell Disease - according to a press release, the:

  • Trial met primary endpoint of hemoglobin response and key secondary endpoints of change from baseline in hemoglobin concentration and indirect bilirubin.
  • Trial showed trend favoring mitapivat but did not meet statistical significance in primary endpoint of annualized rate of SCPCs (pain crises), and the key secondary endpoint of change from baseline in PROMIS Fatigue was not met.

As ever with a set of clinical study results that aren't entirely positive, there is a lot to unpack, but clearly, the market has given data the thumbs down. In this post, we'll dig a little deeper into Agios, it's data, and its competitors to try to see if today's selloff presents a contrarian buy opportunity, or the beginning of a longer-term slide in the share price.

Agios - Background To Today's News - Rationale For My Last Buy call

In a previous post on Agios for Seeking Alpha published in February this year I discussed the history of Agios, from selling its oncology division, to the ups and downs of its development journey with mitapivat, and in September, I reiterated my Buy call on stock, with shares trading at $36.

While mitapivat has secured an approval (in 2022) as a treatment for hemolytic anemia in adults with pyruvate kinase ("PK") deficiency, a rare, debilitating, lifelong hemolytic anemia, Agios reported just $12.9m revenues in Q3, noting that:

149 patients are on therapy in the U.S., inclusive of new starts and continued therapy, representing an increase of 5 percent over the second quarter of 2025.

My Buy thesis was based on the likelihood of the drug gaining approval in the indication of non-transfusion-dependent and transfusion-dependent alpha- or beta-thalassemia . A supplementary New Drug Application ("NDA") was submitted to the FDA, and accepted, based on data from its Phase 3 ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T studies, which met the primary endpoint of transfusion reduction response and all secondary endpoints.

The FDA initially set a PDUFA date of September 7th, 2025, but subsequently data revealed two patients in the studies had experienced acute liver injury, leading to the FDA extending its decision date to 7th December, following Agios' submission of a proposed Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy ("REMS") to mitigate the risk of hepatocellular injury.

I believed an approval was still likely, and the market seemed to concur, pushing Agios' share price up to $45 per share prior to today's selloff.

RISE UP Study Results - Efficacy Misses Bring Results Into Question

The data shared today comes from its pivotal Sickle Cell Disease ("SCD") study. As a quick reminder, as per Agios:

By disrupting hemoglobin production, thalassemia reduces the number of circulating red blood cells and shortens their lifespan, which leads to anemia, fatigue and serious complications.

And, regarding SCD:

Sickle cell disease is a rare, inherited blood disorder caused by the production of abnormal hemoglobin that disrupts the ability of red blood cells to carry oxygen throughout the body. Due to its physical toll, sickle cell disease imposes a profound burden on patients and their families, marked by increased healthcare needs and early mortality.

The mechanism of action ("MoA") of mitapivat in terms of treating SCD is shown in the slide below (taken from today's data presentation by Agios):

The RISE study enrolled 198 patients, who received mitapivat 100mg twice daily, or placebo for 52-weeks, followed by an open label extension period of up to 216 weeks. 85% of patients completed treatments, Agios says, and 174/176 patients who completed initial treatment opted for the OLE study.

The hemoglobin response endpoint was met with a 40.6% response rate in the mitapivat arm, but patients in the mitapivat arm experienced only a 14% reduction in annualized rate of sickle cell pain crises ("SCPCs").

An interpretation from that data may be that the drug ought to have helped patients based on the 40% hemoglobin response, but in reality, proved ineffective at staving off pain crises, i.e., the drug worked in theory but not in practice.

Mitapivat also failed in its key secondary endpoint of change from baseline on a patient-reported fatigue scoring system, also, underlining a lack of efficacy. From a safety perspective, fewer patients experienced grade 3 or above treatment emergent adverse events ("TEAEs") in the mitapivat arm (33.3%) versus the placebo arm (40.6%), and fewer serious TEAEs. Regarding liver events, Agios has shared the following summary:

Looking Ahead - Agios Pushed On, FDA May Push Back - I'm Downgrading to Hold

Agios ends its data summary with the following overview of results:

As we can see, the company plans to press ahead with a marketing submission to the FDA, requesting a further approval for its drug, based on a mixed set of data. The company does have one other asset, noting that a Phase 2b tebapivat trial in lower-risk MDS fully enrolled, with topline results expected in early 2026, but its clear mitapivat is the key value driver.

SCD and TDT are areas of unmet need - Agios notes the recent withdrawals of Novartis (NVS) Adakveo (crianlizumab) from the EU market, owing to a failed study, and withdrawal of Pfizer's (PFE) Oxbrtya (voxelotor) from all markets, on safety concerns - although Agios also notes in its 2024 10K filing:

recently approved treatments for thalassemia, SCD, LR MDS, PKU and PV include Reblozyl from Merck (MRK)/BMS (BMY) (formerly Acceleron/BMS); Revlimid from BMS; Zynteglo and Lyfgenia from bluebird; Adakveo from Novartis; Casgevy from Vertex/CRISPR; Kuvan and Palynziq from BioMarin (BMRN); Endari from Emmaus; Besremi from PharmaEssentia; Jakafi from Incyte (INCY); and Rytelo from Geron (GERN).

Casgevy and Lyfgenia are gene therapies that has produced outstanding clinical study data and secured approval in TDT and SCD, but treatment is expensive and lengthy, and is unlikely to gain widespread use for many more years. Pfizer has given up developing an SCD asset owing to study setbacks, and the remaining drugs listed above bar Adakveo are not indicated for TDT or SCD.

Today's results make the FDA's upcoming decision on whether to grant approval to mitapivat in TDT even more crucial, and potentially may influence the administration's decision. We may note that the EU authorities are expected to make an approval decision for the drug in TDT next year, Agios says.

Casgevy's results in TDT and SCD were both similarly impressive, but in mitapivat's case, it seems it may be effective but potentially unsafe in one indication, and ineffective in another.

These mixed messages make me a little nervous for the TDT approval decision - I almost feel as if, if the agency approves the drug in one indication it will do so in the other, but I am not sure which way it will lean.

Will the FDA recognize the unmet need and feel that having a potentially minimally effective therapy on the market is better than not having it on the market, or will it insist on further studies (which means patients can still receive the drug for free in clinical studies), or will it decide the drug's risk reward profile is not sufficient for approval?

One area in which Agios has few issues is financially, with the company reporting a cash position of $1.26bn as of the end of Q3, although year-to-date net losses of $(350.1m) mean that cash is being rapidly eroded.

If we remove the cash from Agios market cap valuation today of $1.3bn, it suggests the market values the rest of Agios' business at >$50m, which may be a signal that Wall Street has little confidence in mitapivat securing the approvals it needs to drive meaningful (for an ambitious biotech) commercial sales.

With safety issues and efficacy issues now dogging Agios and its hopes for mitapivat, I am downgrading stock to a Hold, because I am uncertain about what the future holds for this business.

I don't think I would sell Agios stock if I held it today, and arguably, there is almost a "free swing" opportunity in play here for a speculative Buy as even if the entire Pyrukund program folds, Agios may still be valued at cash, i.e., no upside on a TDT approval has been baked into the share price - however even if approved, I am not sure Agios will be able to create a significant enough market in the commercial setting, at least not in TDT alone, and not without burning through a lot more cash.

data.title: Malibu Boats Is Cheap, But Industry Conditions Warrant Caution
data.description:
The last few months have been downright painful for shareholders of Malibu Boats (MBUU). The company, which produces and sells recreational power boats, has seen its share price plunge 25.2% since I downgraded the company from a "Buy" to a "Hold" in August of this year. My decision was based on weakening retail demand, the persistence of high interest rates, and an overhang of inventory following the end of the COVID-19 pandemic. After peaking in 2023, revenue, profits, and cash flows started declining. That pain persisted through last year. But now, something else is happening.

Management recently saw a nice jump in revenue as higher prices and an increase in the number of boats sold benefited the business. Shares of the business are trading at levels that I would consider to be very attractive. Having said that, I remain cautious about broader economic conditions. The U.S. economy seems to be heading toward a nasty recession. And even though falling interest rates should eventually bode well for demand, I think it is a bit too early to upgrade the company at this time.

Sticking by the sidelines

The newest data that we have regarding Malibu Boats covers through the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. But first, in the chart above, you can see financial results for the last three fiscal years, with revenue, profits, and cash flows weakening. This came, as the chart below illustrates, as a result of a decline in the number of boats delivered by the company, with only some of that pain offset by a rise in the average revenue per unit sold. The fact of the matter is that weaker consumer confidence, high interest rates, and inflationary pressures created many problems for this space. But now, we are starting to see a recovery here.

In the first chart below, you can see revenue for the first quarter of 2026. Sales for the company totaled $194.7 million. That happens to be 13.5% above the $171.6 million the company reported a year earlier. According to management, this rise in sales can be attributed to a year-over-year improvement in the number of boats delivered, from 1,024 to 1,129. The subsequent chart below shows this, as well as a rise in the average revenue per unit from $167,559 to $172,483.

Management mentioned in their quarterly report that while some of the increase in revenue on a per-boat basis can be chalked up to a favorable model mix associated with its Cobalt segment, another big driver was inflation, with management essentially having to push higher prices onto customers. Even so, demand was clearly resilient, with the company benefiting from a rise in wholesale shipments associated with its Malibu segment. Overall, units there jumped from 384 to 538. This was thanks to lower wholesale shipments last year relative to this year that management blamed on elevated dealer inventory levels. What appears to be happening is that, at least when it comes to this product line, dealers are finally at a point where they feel a bit more comfortable stocking up on boats, presumably because overall inventories have already been normalized.

This does not mean that everything is going great. Management acknowledged in their earnings call that the retail environment for boat sales remains challenged. But to contend with that, the company continues to rely on new product launches, including its Malibu 21 LX, X-axis A200, Cobalt R31 outboard, and its new Pathfinder 2600. This is great in and of itself, but the firm also warned that investors should be prepared for weakness for this year as a whole. At best, we should expect overall revenue to be flat year over year. But it is possible that we could experience a mid-single-digit decline in revenue.

The improvement in revenue brought with it an improvement on the firm's bottom line as well. The net loss for the company went from $5 million to only $0.7 million. Adjusted net profits jumped from $1.7 million to $3.1 million. We saw operating cash flow turn from a negative $8.4 million to a positive $6.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a doubling from $5.4 million to $10.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $9.9 million to $11.8 million.

Unfortunately, it does appear as though profitability will become challenged later this year. Some of this might come from tariff-related costs, with management saying that the estimate right now is for a 1.5% to 3% impact on the firm's cost of sales that has already been incorporated into guidance. Management has said that they have certain mitigation strategies in place, including changing their supply chain, but we should anticipate further price increases as well. As a result of these challenges, the EBITDA margin for the company should be between 8% and 9% for this year. If we assume that a flat to mid single-digit decline for revenue would mean a 2.5% drop in sales, and if we take the midpoint of guidance here for the EBITDA margin for the company, this would imply EBITDA of $66.9 million. This would be down from the $74.8 million the company reported for last year. If we assume similar year-over-year declines for adjusted operating cash flow and adjusted net profits, we would get readings of $59.7 million and $27.6 million, respectively.

With these estimates, as well as the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Although the price-to-adjusted-earnings multiples for the business are a bit lofty, the other multiples look very attractive. And relative to other similar firms, the stock is certainly trading on the cheap. In the table below, I compared it to five other similar enterprises. What I found was that, on both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of these firms was cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, our candidate is the cheapest of the group. It is aided in this regard by the fact that the firm has a net cash position of $21.1 million. This certainly affords it some additional flexibility at a time when market conditions are creating risk for the business.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Malibu Boats 17.1 7.9 6.8
Marine Products Corporation (MPX) 23.6 18.3 17.4
Brunswick Corporation (BC) 101.5 5.9 11.1
MarineMax (HZO) 10.6 50.8 12.5
MasterCraft Boat Holdings (MCFT) 66.7 9.5 12.6
BRP Inc. (DOOO) 26.9 9.5 10.0

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's currently available, I must say that Malibu Boats is looking more appealing by the day. This is even though while the strong first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year is great to see, management anticipates weakening moving forward. Because of how cheap shares are and because of the balance sheet of the company that shows a net cash position as opposed to a net debt position, I would argue that there is certainly some nice potential here. The fact that interest rates are now falling is great for the business. But before we see any real benefit from that, I think we are heading toward a recession. At that point, I suspect that shares will probably be cheaper than they are now. And that would be a great opportunity to buy in. Until that time occurs, however, I think that maintaining a "Hold" rating is the appropriate choice.

data.title: MARA Holdings Is Still Too Vulnerable To Bitcoin Price Fluctuations. Hold.
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Louis Rose as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

My thesis is that MARA Holdings, Inc. (MARA), although more than a simple Bitcoin treasury company, is still too vulnerable to Bitcoin price fluctuations to deserve a higher valuation today. My arguments are that, first of all, the company should be valued as a Bitcoin treasury, a Bitcoin mining company, and a future AI / HPC company. Indeed, their Bitcoin deployment strategy does not contribute meaningfully to their financials, as we will see later, and their recent moves towards AI / HPC, although promising, are still at the proof-of-concept stage. Then, regarding Bitcoin mining, the profitability of their operations is highly dependent on Bitcoin price fluctuations, which is a real risk for the company. Moreover, their capacity for the number of Bitcoins mined in the future is already roughly priced in the stock. Although Bitcoin’s progressive adoption by institutions is a probable long-term catalyst for the stock, I don’t see a reason to attribute them a higher multiple to NAV today. I rate MARA Holdings as a “HOLD”.

Not a Simple Bitcoin Treasury Company

Founded in 2010, MARA Holdings (formerly Marathon Digital Holdings and Marathon Patent Group) originally started as a patent holding company. By the end of the 2010s, the company had completely shifted its line of business to cryptocurrency. Since 2020, the company has operated in Bitcoin mining at scale, being one of the largest public miners by hashrate (meaning mining power) today. In addition to mining, they also buy and hold a very large amount of Bitcoin, being today the second largest bitcoin corporate holder worldwide behind Michael Saylor’s Strategy Inc. (MSTR), formerly Microstrategy. As of November 15th, 2025, MARA owns 53,250 Bitcoins. That’s 0.265% of the 19,948,812 Bitcoins mined to date, and 0.254% of all the 21,000,000 Bitcoins that will ever be mined.

At the time of writing, Bitcoin’s price of $92,900 values MARA’s Bitcoin reserve at $4.95 billion. If we subtract from that their $3.52 billion in total long-term liabilities and add back their $0.82 billion in cash and cash equivalents (see their 10-Q here), we obtain a $2.25 billion theoretical market capitalization, solely from their Bitcoin reserve. Their current market capitalization of $4.41 billion implies a mNAV (multiple-to-net-asset-value) of 1.96x. This means that MARA Holdings currently trades at a 96% premium compared to the net value of their Bitcoin holdings.

This premium is explained by the fact that, contrary to Strategy, MARA Holdings is not a simple Bitcoin treasury company. As we have seen, they mine Bitcoin: over the last 5 quarters, around 2000 to 2500 Bitcoins per quarter. They also actively deploy their Bitcoin holdings: in loans (19.7% of all Bitcoin holdings) or as collateral for credit lines (9.6%). Finally, they are now expanding into AI/HPC (High Performance Computing). They plan on diversifying their computing power use in private cloud computing (More specifically, in edge AI inference). Their recent acquisition (pending regulatory approval) of a 64% stake in Paris-based startup Exaion for $168M supports this strategic shift, as Exaion has expertise in tier III/IV data center management and AI/HPC.

With respect to their mining operations, they are switching from an asset-light model to a vertically integrated model, where they directly own a growing part of the full mining pipeline: the energy production facility, the data center and the Bitcoin mining equipment. Back in 2023, 97% of their capacity was hosted at third-party sites. In Q3 2025, that share was reduced to 49%: 1099 of all the 2144 Bitcoins produced during the quarter were from their own operations. As stated in their Q325 shareholder letter, this strategic shift from an asset-light model to a vertically integrated model does negatively impact their current expenses — namely, their capital expenditures and general and administrative expenditures, see their 10-Q — but improves their cash-flow generation prospects. Indeed, as MARA Holdings phases out third-party contracts over time, they expect their Bitcoin mining unitary costs to continue to decrease. Thanks to this ongoing strategic pivot, they have already reduced their cost per petahash per day by 15% between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025, which helped them reach a purchased energy cost per Bitcoin of $39.2 thousand.

Their declared goal is to reduce the third-party sites' contribution to 30% by the end of 2025. To continue to make progress towards that goal, they recently entered into a partnership with MPLX LP (formerly known as Marathon Petroleum) to gain direct access to the Delaware Basin energy resources (natural gas). MARA will build and operate power plants on-site that will convert the natural gas to electricity, which will power both MARA data centers and MPLX operations.

Regarding their diversification into AI and High Performance Computing, MARA deployed their first 10 AI inference racks at the Granbury, Texas site in Q3 2025 (see the Q3 earnings presentation, slide 5). This is the beginning of their effort to leverage their infrastructure for AI applications. As a 255% increase in AI power demand is forecasted between 2025 and 2030, MARA Holdings strategically positions itself at the confluence of energy, compute, cryptocurrency and AI.

MARA’s Bitcoin Deployment Strategy Isn’t a Significant Contributor to Earnings

MARA’s Bitcoin deployment strategy encompasses loans (19.7% of all Bitcoins), active management (3.6% of all Bitcoins) and credit collaterals (9.6% of all Bitcoins).

First, Bitcoin’s use as a credit collateral is simply a financing activity and does not contribute to profits per se. Then, the active management line of business can also be considered non-meaningful, as only a very small portion (3.6%) of owned Bitcoins is concerned. The main contributor to their deployment strategy is the loans: in Q3 2025, MARA declared an interest income of $17.67 million. When dividing this $17.67 million interest income by the 10,377 loaned Bitcoins, this equates to an annual yield of 5% to 7% depending on Bitcoin’s market price, which is in line with standard yields for the practice. Still, MARA’s $17.67 million interest income has to be compared to their interest expenses of $12.76 million for the quarter: their net interest income is thus $4.91 million.

I state that this cannot be a significant contribution to their market capitalization. Even when attributing a generous 30x P/E ratio, this would only contribute $147 million to their market capitalization, which is currently $4.53 billion. As such, I see MARA’s Bitcoin deployment strategy as a way to comfortably cover their interest expenses, but not much more than this. I conclude that this line of business should not be considered as a meaningful contributor to their market capitalization.

MARA’s Diversification Into AI is Promising, but Still at the Proof-of-Concept Stage

In their Q3 2025 shareholder letter, MARA Holdings states that their energy expertise is a strategic advantage in this new era of Artificial Intelligence, where growing AI demand will be constrained by the energy supply. MARA’s long-term goal is to allocate a portion of their computing power to AI inference on the edge. The day-to-day repartition of computing power between cryptocurrency mining and AI inference will depend on where the higher profitability lies. My argument is that, while having a lot of potential, this diversification into AI is still at the proof-of-concept stage today, as the numbers will show. As such, the potential of diversification into AI inference can only be partially accounted for in the stock price to date.

In Q3 2025, MARA Holdings installed ten AI racks within a non-water-cooled modular data center at its Granbury, Texas site, which has a 225 MW operational capacity. For reference, MARA Holdings' total operational capacity across all sites culminates at 1.2 GW. Although MARA did not communicate the exact power capacity allocated to AI inference at the Granbury, Texas site, we can make a rough estimation for it. Today, modern GPU racks consume 10 to 15 kW per rack. This means that, currently, MARA’s AI inference operations only absorb 0.12 MW (average estimate) out of the 1200 MW available. Meaning, about 0.1% of the total available power.

For MARA Holdings, this first step into AI diversification echoes the recent strategic shifts from other, more advanced Bitcoin miners: TeraWulf, backed by Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) or Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ). According to this VanEck 2024 report, if Bitcoin miners were to allocate 20% of their electrical capacity to AI, this could justify a doubling of the stock price. This is explained by the fact that AI workloads can generate more revenue than Bitcoin mining operations, while reducing exposure to Bitcoin's price and reducing infrastructure costs.

Bitcoin miners have a time-to-market advantage: repurposing a Bitcoin mining site into an AI data center takes, on average, about a year, while building one from scratch takes 3 to 6 years. Regarding profitability potential, the Core Scientific example is revealing: they contracted a total revenue of $6.7B over 12 years with CoreWeave, for a final capacity projected at 500 MW. For Core Scientific, this entails several advantages: a reduced exposure to Bitcoin's price, a guaranteed revenue for 12 years, and having infrastructure upgrades paid by the customer.

Considering that MARA Holdings is very early in its AI diversification pivot, and is late compared to a number of competitors, I only attribute the company a 30% premium for its AI diversification plans.

MARA’s Bitcoin Mining Activity Alone Does Not Justify its Current Valuation

Between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025, MARA mined approximately 9200 Bitcoins. Let’s analyze the profitability of their mining activity (specifically for their fully-owned sites, which should represent the vast majority of their mining activity as soon as 2026). The chart below also shows Bitcoin purchases by MARA Holdings, which are independent from their mining business. Large-scale cryptocurrency purchases are simply a bet on the coin’s future price, and fall under the bitcoin treasury part of their business.

Let’s estimate the profitability of MARA’s mining business. MARA’s bitcoin production fluctuates between 2000 and 2500 Bitcoins per quarter. They are the largest publicly traded Bitcoin miner, with an energized hashrate of 60.4 EH/s. For clarification, 1 EH/s means one quintillion hash computations per second. The total network hashrate for Bitcoin is around 1100 EH/s. This means that MARA Holdings contributes about 5.5% of all computing power allocated to Bitcoin mining worldwide.

For their wholly-owned sites specifically, the energy purchase cost is $39.2 thousand per Bitcoin, one of the lowest in the sector. But several other costs must be accounted for. Operating and maintenance costs, as well as general and administrative costs. Using their 10-Q numbers, operating and maintenance costs came at $12.9 thousand per Bitcoin in Q3 2025. Regarding general & administrative costs, I state that most, but not all, of those costs should be attributed to the Bitcoin mining activity. Let’s take a lenient hypothesis and allocate only 70% of general and administrative costs to their mining activity. This leads to a total cost per Bitcoin mined of $79.3 thousand. Using the current Bitcoin price of $95,750, the cash operating margin is 20.7% (including 70% of general and administrative expenses). Were we to attribute 90% of general and administrative expenses to mining, the break-even price per Bitcoin would be $88 thousand, and the operating margin would fall to 9%.

As we can see, the Bitcoin mining margins are highly dependent on Bitcoin price fluctuations. If the Bitcoin price were to drop below $80K for a sustained period, this could spell trouble for the company, which would still have to finance its various expenses. To support the argument that expenses financing is a potential risk for MARA, the company recently declared that “they may now choose to sell a portion of the Bitcoin produced from mining to support their ongoing operating expenses” (see page 14 of the Q3 2025 shareholder letter).

We also have to consider that Bitcoin mining tends to become more difficult over time: the number of remaining Bitcoins to mine steadily shrinks, mining equipment depreciates and becomes obsolete, total network hashrate tends to grow over time due to increasing competition, and successive halvings reduce the number of Bitcoins granted per block won.

If MARA Holdings were to keep their 5.5% share of total Bitcoin mining compute over time, they would obtain about 57,800 Bitcoins out of the remaining 1,051,800 to mine. That would increase their current Bitcoin reserve by 108%. If we take into account the costs associated with mining those Bitcoins, and assume a 20% cash operating margin, MARA would make $1.07B in profit by mining those remaining 57,800 Bitcoins at current market prices. That’s a x1.47 bonus from their current Net Asset Value. When taking into account the 30% valuation premium from the AI diversification shift, I estimate that MARA Holdings currently deserves a x1.91 multiple to NAV. They are currently selling at a x1.96 multiple. I conclude that MARA Holdings' current valuation already prices in the Bitcoin mining business, as well as their diversification into AI.

MARA’s Net Income Evolution Corroborates my Thesis

When looking at MARA Holdings' Net Income, the overall thesis is verified: the net income is highly volatile, sometimes negative, and depends mostly on Bitcoin’s price movements.

For example, in Q3 2025, MARA declared a Net Income of $123.1M. But Bitcoin’s price increase from June 30, 2025, to September 30, 2025, actually contributed $343M to their quarterly net income. If the Bitcoin market price had been flat during the quarter, MARA’s Net Income would actually have been negative. This supports the thesis that MARA Holdings' financial performance is highly reliant on Bitcoin’s price appreciation over time, and that the company does not generate significant value aside from holding Bitcoin that appreciates over time, and mining Bitcoin at a positive but highly volatile profit margin.

Conclusion

I conclude that MARA Holdings' current market capitalization correctly reflects the current value of their Bitcoin holdings, of their Bitcoin mining business and of their future growth potential from AI/HPC diversification. As discussed, their Bitcoin deployment strategy does not constitute a significant contribution to their earnings power to date. Although the long-term trend for Bitcoin is upwards as institutional adoption progresses worldwide, MARA Holdings' financial health is still highly dependent on short-term and medium-term Bitcoin price fluctuations. As such, I consider MARA Holdings to be fairly valued currently. I would need to see more execution on the AI diversification side, as well as a reduced exposure to Bitcoin's price, before rerating the stock. My recommendation: HOLD.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Neutron Delay Creates A Better Entry
data.description:
Investment Rating - Buy

Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) is a mid-cap space tech company from Long Beach, California, that is engaged in launch services and space systems solutions, and it's better known for its Electron (an orbital small launch vehicle for small spacecraft launch services) as well as Neutron (launch vehicles for large constellation deployments). The RKLB stock price didn't move much after its post-2021 correction, when many hyper-growth companies experienced massive pullbacks in terms of their valuation multiples. But since mid-2024, RKLB quickly became a 10-bagger as its market cap went up from $2.5-3.0 billion up to $30 billion (by mid-October).

As of mid-November, we see that RKLB is correcting, and it has already lost over 40%, which is frightening some investors. However, I think that for the long term, RKLB looks very promising, and what might have added to the weakness – Neutron's more conservative schedule – is actually improving the risk/reward story here after this 40%+ correction. From what I saw in the company's recent operational and financial updates for fiscal Q3, the execution was there, and the business is getting more diverse and stable with multiple levers for future margin expansion. I initiate my coverage with a "Buy" rating, although the stock price might still look a bit too expensive.

Rocket Lab's Q3 Earnings Discussion

In its Q3 2025 report, which came out a few days ago, RKLB reported $155 million in revenues, which were up +48% and +7% on YoY and QoQ bases, respectively, and it was about 2.22% above the consensus this time. The non-GAAP gross margin hit 41.9%, which was great to see. But what really helped RKLB narrow the earnings loss in Q3 (from negative 10 cents last year to negative 3 cents as of Q3 2025) was tax benefits. As a result, Rocket Lab managed to beat the EPS consensus by the largest magnitude since early 2022.

The stock initially reacted with a positive move up, but it then faded when the management announced that 1) the Neutron's first launch will slip into 2026, and 2) the total Neutron program cost will be up to ~$360 million (vs. prior $275 million guidance). Management noted that they're expecting incremental spending ahead with about $15 million of labor cost (per quarter), and that's where that increase comes from.

The $250 million to $300 million kind of original estimate, we kind of -- that got a little bit, I would say, behind us kind of with the push from launching middle of '25 to the end of '25. And so now as we get into kind of a 2026 scenario, right now, I'd say that we're estimating that we will have spent approximately $360 million exiting in cumulative across R&D and CapEx through the end of 2025.

From RKLB's Q3 2025 earnings call

Aside from the Neutron news, what saved the day for RKLB is that its backlog reached $1.1 billion, with about 57% of it set to turn into revenues in the coming 12 months or so. And they were holding ~$976 million in cash and marketable securities as of September 30, 2025, which was over 2.3x more than last year, so in terms of liquidity, that expected acceleration in both labor costs and Neutron R&D looks manageable to me.

What seems to be the most important indicator for any growth company is its product fit, and, on this front, RKLB is winning. In Q3 there were 17 dedicated Electron launches booked in the quarter, which was the highest ever (for a single quarter), and 15 out of those 17 launches were booked for international customers in Japan, Korea, and Europe. Rocket Lab is broadening its reach beyond just the United States, and it suggests to me that its offerings are superior to other global options. The company, despite its relatively small size, silently becomes the default small‑launch and responsive‑launch provider outside SpaceX, especially for allies who want non‑Falcon options but don't have sovereign vehicles in place.

Basically, every new space company, except Rocket Lab and SpaceX has failed to build an orbital rocket that is scaled to any kind of launch cadence and is reliable.

From RKLB's Q3 2025 earnings call

Another important thing that we saw recently was Rocket Lab's HASTE missions. Two of them flew in Q3. In November 2025, another HASTE mission, which was launched within 14 months of contract signing, carried payloads for the Missile Defense Agency and Defense Innovation Unit. It's an important achievement for RKLB because HASTE uses the Electron architecture but is tailored for hypersonic testing, so it has higher ASPs. If the management keeps signing such new launches, it can dramatically improve the profitability side and help RKLB break even faster than it's currently expected by the market's consensus.

Management noted that Electron cadence is reaching a structural step‑up. The production system was built to support up to 52 rockets/year, and today's cadence is still far below that ceiling.

Electron's like 90% plus built in-house. So we don't see too many challenges there. The factory that we built here was ultimately designed to build 52 rockets a year.

From RKLB's Q3 2025 earnings call

Once it rises, the fixed costs are not going to increase that much, and each incremental launch will then carry a higher gross margin ahead.

The Space Systems (SS) segment, which designs and builds the "things" that go into space, should provide structurally higher and more stable margins than the launch business. The segment accounts for ~53% of the company's aggregate $1.1 billion backlog as of Q3, so the market's fixation on rockets may be limited and not reflecting the true potential. With Geost (electro‑optical and infrared sensors), SolAero (solar), and Mynaric (laser communications) layered on top of Rocket Lab’s existing platforms and components, management can now bid for missions as the lead integrator, which is a great competitive advantage over smaller peers, who used to sell either buses or niche components without system‑level integration. This is where I see moat formation for RKLB ahead, so to speak, because such vertical integration lets Rocket Lab stack margins. They've already reached 30%+ in SolAero's gross margin, seeing other components can run in the 40–60%+ range.

On the Space Systems components or subsystems business, that has a wide range with solar kind of being at the lowest end of that and again, around 30 points. Hopefully, we can push that a little bit higher. And for some of our other components business, we have margins that are well north of 60, in some cases, 70 points of margin. And I think overall, that kind of brings the gross market profile for that subsystems business around, call it, low to mid-40s.

From RKLB's Q3 2025 earnings call

The existing Space Systems backlog plus Electron/HASTE demand support a credible path to continued double‑digit growth, even if Neutron's launch is shifted again. So, overall, I like RKLB's growth story and its prospects for future business model validation amid rising gross-level margins and, of course, continued top-line growth.

Neutron's More Conservative Schedule Is Okay

Before the Q3 release, management was targeting late 2025 for the first Neutron vehicle launch, but now we know that this timeline has shifted toward Q1 2026. I think that the delay was necessary because the downside of potentially rushing at launch, when they're not confident about the system's readiness, would destroy vastly more value than an extra 1–2 quarters of additional spending. What I liked to read in the earnings call transcript was that RKLB is reaching peak Neutron R&D spending, so it gives hope for some possible margin expansion in the next two years.

I'm optimistic that with the impressive strides we've made towards this milestone, we're approaching peak Neutron R&D spending and are on the path towards meaningful operating leverage and positive cash flow in the future.

From RKLB's Q3 2025 earnings call

In terms of future financials, the way I see it, the slip we saw doesn't meaningfully impair Neutron's demand opportunity ahead. Management probably knows that the U.S. Space Force requires a successful flight before awarding missions, although the awards themselves aren't tied to the old late‑2025 internal date. So, RKLB still envisions a test launch first (fully expensed R&D), then revenue launches thereafter (Three missions in the first 12 months post‑debut).

RKLB's Valuation Looks Rich, But It's Not A Reason To Pass

When RKLB's Q3 came out, we saw that the market didn't punish the firm with negative revisions (at least there were only positive adjustments to the long-term estimates).

Based on the FY 2027 consensus, RKLB is trading at almost 19x forward sales, which makes the stock look very expensive, and it explains the post-Q3 reaction quite well. Given that the Neutron launch was shifted, even for 1 quarter, the new guidance affected market expectations, and the fragility of high valuation multiples pressed the stock price heavily.

However, I believe that we can assume higher sales by FY 2027 compared to the current estimate because the strength in the SS segment, plus the likely Neutron launch and its further commercialization, should lead to more contracted launches. Putting a 20% premium on top of FY 2027 sales looks like a reasonable assumption to me.

RKLB is one of a kind without SpaceX's public presence, and I think this fact gives the stock a higher multiple even among similarly growing tech stocks. At 20x FY 2027 sales multiple, I think that RKLB's market cap should reach $29–30 billion in the next 12–24 months. This gives me an upside of about 29%. And if we extend the premium on RKLB's sales growth ahead, both the multiples and the absolute sales amount may be higher than the outputs I received above.

Risks to My Views

The main risk, as we could have seen after RKLB's Q3 print, is the execution on what the market initially reflected in the consensus. While I believe that Rocket Lab will be able to cope with the ongoing challenges and come out of the rising expenses spree, the stock looks risky, and it shouldn't be viewed as a no-brainer even for experienced growth investors. We should carefully monitor the ongoing progress on the Neutron developments and the SS business currents. Any further delays in the Neutron launch or any negative guidance revisions for margins or new orders will likely trigger another correction cycle.

The stock is still very expensive, based on conventional valuation metrics. RKLB's FY 2027 sales multiple is ~19x, which is a lot, and it reflects that there are many bulls in the market waiting for a better day ahead. If such better days don't come, or they are delayed, the bulls might want to close their positions en masse. In this case, RKLB can easily lose another 40% of its market cap in just a few weeks.

Conclusion

I think that the Neutron delay has shaped a better entry point for RKLB investors after the post-Q3 correction. I like the operational progress that is taking place, and I truly believe that RKLB's rich valuation isn't a reason to pass by. I expect RKLB's comeback to its previous highs, so I rate it as a "Buy."

data.title: Columbia Convertible Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Convertible securities performed very well during the third quarter, boosting their year-to-date return as of September 30 to 15.70%, as measured by the ICE BofA US Convertibles Index. Markets continued to rebound from the tariff-fueled lows of early April, amid positive economic growth and mounting optimism about the outlook for U.S. Federal Reserve policy. Convertibles further benefited from the strong representation of issuers linked to the types of high-growth themes that were in favor, such as artificial intelligence (AI) and data centers, aerospace/defense and cryptocurrencies.

Positive market momentum was reflected in robust issuance, with 52 new issues coming to market during the quarter, for a total value of $34 billion. In comparison, the third quarter of 2024 saw only 19 new issues valued at $11 billion. Many of the convertibles issued in the past three months rallied sharply in their initial weeks of trading, indicating the strength in investor demand. Although issuance was broad-based, the technology sector was heavily represented at approximately 40% of the quarter’s new convertible issues.

Quarterly portfolio recap

The fund outperformed its benchmark during the quarter, adding to its advantage in the year-to-date period. We typically position the portfolio to capture market upside, rather than emphasizing downside risk management. This approach has generally worked well over time, and it continued to hold the fund in good stead over the past three months. We further benefited from our participation in the new-issue market throughout 2025 so far, as it allowed the fund to take advantage of strength in this area.

At the sector level, our holdings outperformed their corresponding benchmark components by the widest margins in health care, consumer discretionary and information technology. On the other hand, we experienced the weakest relative performance in real estate. Among individual positions, Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE) -a producer of energy storage solutions whose securities we purchased when they were issued in June- was a top performer. Eos Energy securities more than doubled in price during the quarter, reflecting continued enthusiasm about companies poised to benefit from providing energy needed to power Al. The power theme also benefited Centrus Energy, a producer of enriched uranium for the nuclear power industry. Lumentum Holdings, a designer of products for optical networking and laser applications, was another top performer.

Consistent with the favorable market environment, the majority of the largest detractors from relative performance were securities in which the fund was underweight or held zero weightings. For example, we missed the rally in Opendoor Technologies, whose convertibles gained more than 600% as its common-stock shares achieved “meme stock” status. We were also hurt somewhat by not holding positions in certain individual Al-related issues including Applied Digital and TeraWulf, as we sought to maintain Al exposure through holdings in other issuers.

Outlook

While we don't anticipate the market rally can sustain its pace of the past six months, we continue to have a positive view on convertibles. We typically assess valuations by looking at delta, or the convertible market's sensitivity to stock price movements. We view a delta in the high 30s to low 40s as being very compelling, while we usually become more cautious once the delta moves above 60. The market's delta stood at 53 at the end of September, up from 47 at the beginning of the quarter and a low of approximately 40 during the tariff-driven sell-off of early April. We think this indicates room for additional upside, especially in light of the accelerating pace of innovation across the U.S. economy. We believe these factors, in combination, indicate that the convertible market continues to offer fertile ground for bottom-up security selection.
